Intro1: Cognitive reflection is the ability to pause and question an immediate answer before responding. It helps people make better decisions when a first impression feels right but may be wrong (Frederick, 2005; Toplak, West, & Stanovich, 2014).
Intro2: A helpful way to think about this comes from Daniel Kahneman’s Nobel Prize-winning work on judgment and decision-making. He described two broad modes of thought: a fast mode that is automatic and effortless, and a slower mode that is deliberate and effortful. Fast thinking is often useful, but it can also produce confident mistakes when a problem looks simple but needs checking (Kahneman, 2011). Cognitive reflection is the habit of noticing that moment and slowing down.
Intro3: This matters in the AI age because AI systems often produce answers that sound fluent and convincing. That can make it easier to accept the first plausible response instead of examining it. If people rely too heavily on external tools they may make a lot of mistakes, and get fewer chances to practice checking their own reasoning.
Intro4: The good news is that cognitive reflection can be strengthened through practice. Short reasoning exercises can build the habit of testing an initial answer before committing to it. Over time, this can improve judgment and help people stay alert to common reasoning traps (Frederick, 2005; Toplak et al., 2014; Kahneman, 2011).
Ref_Frederick: Frederick, S. (2005). Cognitive reflection and decision making. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 19(4), 25–42.
Ref_Toplak: Toplak, M. E., West, R. F., & Stanovich, K. E. (2014). Assessing miserly information processing: An expansion of the Cognitive Reflection Test. Thinking and Reasoning, 20(2), 147–168.
Ref_Kahneman: Kahneman, D. (2011). Thinking, Fast and Slow. New York, NY: Farrar, Straus and Giroux.
